content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Chris Nance 
2928 Latham Drive 
Sacramento, CA 95864 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-20-019 
Dear Mr. Nance: 
February 20, 2020 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the post-government employment provisions of the Act and not under any other post-
government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code Section· 10411. Also, we are not a 
finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 ·FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we 
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
( 1) Does the One-Year Ban prohibit you, the former Chief Communications Officer of the 
California Earthquake Authority ("CEA"), from appearing before or communicating with the 
California Office of Emergency Services ("Cal OES") or its officers or employees in regard to a 
proposal for a Cal OES contract in which your consulting firm would serve as a subcontractor? 
(2) Does the Permanent Ban prohibit you from working on the Cal OES contract at issue as 
a subcontractor? 
CONCLUSIONS 
(1) No. The One-Year Ban does not prohibit you from appearing before or communicating 
with Cal OES or its officers or employees because, based on the facts presented, Cal OES is not 
your former state administrative agency, you were not loaned to Cal OES by CEA and did not work 
for or represent Cal OES in the 12 months prior to leaving state service, and Cal OES's budget, 
personnel, and other operations are not subject to CEA's direction and control. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through.18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-20-019 
Page No. 2 
(2) No. The Permanent Ban does not prohibit you from working on the Cal OES contract at 
issue because you did not participate in any manner in the making of that contract prior to leaving 
state service based on the facts presented. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the former Chief Communications Officer of the CEA, a position designated in the 
CEA's Conflict oflnterest Code. Your position is not designated in either the Governor's Office's 
Conflict of Interest Code or Cal OES's Conflict of Interest Code. 
On January 3, 2020, you signed off from all CEA systems, had the CEA website updated to 
remove you from being shown as a member of the CEA executive team, and left state service. Due 
to your accrual ofleave credits while employed by the state, however, you will remain on the 
CEA's payroll through the end of February 2020. Since leaving state service, you have opened your 
own consulting firm, and you intend for the firm to be a subcontractor on an advertising agency's 
proposal to be the general contractor for a Cal OES contract. Proposals for the contract are 
scheduled to be presented to Cal OES on February 21, 2020, and Cal OES is scheduled to award the 
contract on March 2, 2020. The Director of Cal OES is the Governor's current designee to the CEA 
Governing Board. 
You provided additional facts on February 12, 2020. You state that you did not participate in 
any manner in the making of the Cal OES contract prior to leaving state service. Your consulting 
firm's work pursuing the subcontract will not be compensated unless Cal OES selects the contract 
proposal which includes your consulting firm as a subcontractor. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the One-Year Ban and the Permanent Ban. 
The One-Year Ban 
The "One-Year Ban" prohibits certain former state employees, including a former 
"designated employee" of a "state administrative agency" such as yourself, from appearing before 
or communicating with his or her former agency or its officers or employees on behalf of any other 
person for compensation for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action or 
any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) As the former Chief Communications Officer of the CEA, a position designated in the 
CEA's Conflict oflnterest Code, you are subject-to the One-Year Ban. 
Date on which You Permanently Left State Service? 
We first consider when you left state service for purposes of the One-Year Ban. Regulation 
18746.4(b) provides that the date on which an officer of a state administrative agency permanently 
leaves office or employment is the date on which the officer is no longer authorized to perform the 
duties of the office or employment, and stops performing_those duties, despite the officer continuing 


File No. A-20-019 
Page No. 3 
to receive compensation for accrued leave credits thereafter. You stopped performing the duties of 
the CEA's Chief Communications Officer on January 3, 2020 based on the facts presented. 
Therefore, you permanently left state service on that date, and you are subject to the One-Year Ban 
until January 3, 2021. 
For Compensation on Behalf of Another Person? 
We next consider whether your contemplated appearance before or communication with Cal 
OES regarding the Cal OES contract at issue would be "for compensation" and "on behalf of any 
other person" for purposes of the One-Year Ban. As noted above, the One-Year Ban prohibits an 
appearance or communication only if it is for compensation or promised compensation and on 
behalf of another person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
Regulation 18746.l(b)(4), however, further provides that "[a]n appearance or 
communication made by a public official solely to represent his or her personal interests ... is not 
prohibit~d or limited by this section." For this purpose, "personal interests" include "[a] business 
entity owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and 
members of his or her immediate family" and "[a] business entity over which the official, members 
of his or her immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or 
jointly exercise full direction and control." (Regulation 18704(d)(2)(B) and (C).) Thus, the One 
Year Ban does not prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with your former state 
administrative agency if you are solely representing your personal interest. 
However, you state that you intend for your consulting firm to serve as a subcontractor as 
part of an advertising agency's proposal to be the general contractor on the Cal OES contract at 
issue. For a former official's appearance before or communication with his or her former state 
administrative agency associated with a subcontract, the subcontract is not with the former agency; 
instead, the subcontract is with the general contractor, and the former official would, in essence, be 
working for and paid by the general contractor. Thus, although the subcontract would not be 
prohibited by the Act, the One Year Ban would prohibit a former official from appearing before or 
communicating with a former agency in connection with business involving a subcontract because 
the subcontract would be "for compensation" and "on behalf of any other person." (Woolley Advice 
Letter, No. A-14-164.) 
Does the One-Year Ban Apply to You with Respect to Cal OES? 
Accordingly, we next consider whether the One-Year Ban would apply to you with respect 
to an appearance before or communicatioII with Cal OES or its officers or employees. The One-
Year Ban applies to a former official's appearance before or communication with any of the 
following state administrative agencies and those agencies' officers or employees: the agency the 
former official worked for or represented; any agency the former official worked for or represented 
while on loan from the former official's agency; and any agency whose budget, personnel, and 


File No. A-20-019 
Page No. 4 
other operations are under the control of the former official's agency.2 (Regulation 
18746.1 (b )(6)(C).) 
The facts presented provide no indication that CEA loaned you to Cal OES, or that you 
· worked for or represented Cal OES in the 12 months prior to leaving state employment. Nor do they 
provide any ipdication that CEA has direction and control over Cal OES's budget, personnel, and 
other operations. While the Director of Cal OES is the Governor's current designee to the CEA 
Governing Board, this indicates only that Cal OES may have some influence over CEA, not that the 
CEA has any direction and control of Cal OES. Therefore, the One-Year Ban does not apply to your 
potential appearance before or communication with Cal OES or its officers or employees regarding 
the Cal OES contract at issue based on the facts presented. 
The Permanent Ban 
The "Permanent Ban" on "switching sides" prohibits a former "state administrative 
official,"3 such as yourself, from working for compensation on certain proceedings in which the 
official participated while working for the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
The Permanent Ban is a lifetime ban which applies to any formal or informal appearance or any oral 
or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing 
any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or communication - made 
with the intent to influence any judicial, quasHudicial, or other proceeding in which you 
participated while you served as a state administrative official. (Ibid.) 
'"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... " (Section 87400(c).) An official is considered to have "participated" in a 
proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally and substantially" prior to leaving 
state service. (Section 87400(d).) 
You state that you did not participate in any mariner in making the Cal OES contract at issue 
prior to leaving state service. Therefore, the Permanent Ban does not apply to you with respect to 
that Cal OES contract based on the facts presented. 
2 The One-Year Ban also prohibits a former official from appearing before or communicating with any state 
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, subject to the direction and control of the Governor if the official 
was a designated employee ofthe Governor's Office. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6)(C)(iii).) This provision is not 
applicable because the CEA's Chief Communications Officer is not designated in the Governor's Office's Conflict of 
Interest Code, and because the facts presented provide no indication you held any other position with the state. 
3 Section 87400(b) defines "state administrative official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of 
a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or 
other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." 


File No. A-20-019 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
. By: 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
tl;;ftfd;/ 
~atthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

a subcontractor?

(1) No. The One-Year Ban does not prohibit you from appearing before or communicating 
with Cal OES or its officers or employees because, based on the facts presented, Cal OES is not 
your former state administrative agency, you were not loaned to Cal OES by CEA and did not work 
for or represent Cal OES in the 12 months prior to leaving state service, and Cal OES's budget, 
personnel, and other operations are not subject to CEA's direction and control.(2) No. The Permanent Ban does not prohibit you from working on the Cal OES contract at 
issue because you did not participate in any manner in the making of that contract prior to leaving 
state service based on the facts presented.

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the One-Year Ban and the Permanent Ban. 
The One-Year Ban 
The "One-Year Ban" prohibits certain former state employees, including a former 
"designated employee" of a "state administrative agency" such as yourself, from appearing before 
or communicating with his or her former agency or its officers or employees on behalf of any other

leaves office or employment is the date on which the officer is no longer authorized to perform the

the CEA's Chief Communications Officer on January 3, 2020 based on the facts presented.

We next consider whether your contemplated appearance before or communication with Cal

appearance or communication only if it is for compensation or promised compensation and on 
behalf of another person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Section 87406;

Regulation 18746.l(b)(4), however, further provides that "[a]n appearance or 
communication made by a public official solely to represent his or her personal interests ... is not 
prohibit~d or limited by this section." For this purpose, "personal interests" include "[a] business 
entity owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and 
members of his or her immediate family" and "[a] business entity over which the official, members 
of his or her immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or 
jointly exercise full direction and control." (Regulation 18704(d)(2)(B) and (C).) Thus, the One 
Year Ban does not prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with your former state 
administrative agency if you are solely representing your personal interest. 
However, you state that you intend for your consulting firm to serve as a subcontractor as 
part of an advertising agency's proposal to be the general contractor on the Cal OES contract at 
issue. For a former official's appearance before or communication with his or her former state

instead, the subcontract is with the general contractor, and the former official would, in essence, be

the subcontract would be "for compensation" and "on behalf of any other person." (Woolley Advice 
Letter, No. A-14-164.)

Accordingly, we next consider whether the One-Year Ban would apply to you with respect 
to an appearance before or communicatioII with Cal OES or its officers or employees. The One-
Year Ban applies to a former official's appearance before or communication with any of the 
following state administrative agencies and those agencies' officers or employees: the agency the 
former official worked for or represented; any agency the former official worked for or represented 
while on loan from the former official's agency; and any agency whose budget, personnel, and  
other operations are under the control of the former official's agency.2 (Regulation 
18746.1 (b )(6)(C).) 
The facts presented provide no indication that CEA loaned you to Cal OES, or that you 
· worked for or represented Cal OES in the 12 months prior to leaving state employment. Nor do they 
provide any ipdication that CEA has direction and control over Cal OES's budget, personnel, and 
other operations. While the Director of Cal OES is the Governor's current designee to the CEA 
Governing Board, this indicates only that Cal OES may have some influence over CEA, not that the 
CEA has any direction and control of Cal OES. Therefore, the One-Year Ban does not apply to your

the Cal OES contract at issue based on the facts presented.

The "Permanent Ban" on "switching sides" prohibits a former "state administrative 
official,"3 such as yourself, from working for compensation on certain proceedings in which the

You state that you did not participate in any mariner in making the Cal OES contract at issue 
prior to leaving state service. Therefore, the Permanent Ban does not apply to you with respect to 
that Cal OES contract based on the facts presented. 
2 The One-Year Ban also prohibits a former official from appearing before or communicating with any state 
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, subject to the direction and control of the Governor if the official 
was a designated employee ofthe Governor's Office. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(6)(C)(iii).) This provision is not

3 Section 87400(b) defines "state administrative official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of 
a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or 
other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity."

QUESTION: ( 1) Does the One-Year Ban prohibit you, the former Chief Communications Officer of the 
California Earthquake Authority ("CEA"), from appearing before or communicating with the 
California Office of Emergency Services ("Cal OES") or its officers or employees in regard to a 
proposal for a Cal OES contract in which your consulting firm would serve as a subcontractor? 
(2) Does the Permanent Ban prohibit you from working on the Cal OES contract at issue as 
a subcontractor?

CONCLUSION: (1) No. The One-Year Ban does not prohibit you from appearing before or communicating 
with Cal OES or its officers or employees because, based on the facts presented, Cal OES is not 
your former state administrative agency, you were not loaned to Cal OES by CEA and did not work 
for or represent Cal OES in the 12 months prior to leaving state service, and Cal OES's budget, 
personnel, and other operations are not subject to CEA's direction and control.(2) No. The Permanent Ban does not prohibit you from working on the Cal OES contract at 
issue because you did not participate in any manner in the making of that contract prior to leaving 
state service based on the facts presented.
